short_description: GitLab CI/CD runners unresponsive — all pipelines queued
description: All 8 GitLab CI/CD shared runners are showing as 'offline' in the GitLab admin panel. 120+ pipelines are stuck in 'pending' state. Development velocity is completely halted. Issue started at approximately 14:30 UTC.
close_notes:
ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS:
The gitlab-runner service on all 8 runner VMs stopped responding after an automatic OS security patch (applied via RHEL Satellite at 14:15 UTC) updated the 'nftables' package, which reset firewall rules and blocked outbound connections from the runners to the GitLab server on port 443.

RESOLUTION STEPS TAKEN:
1. Checked runner status in GitLab Admin → Runners panel — all showed last contact    timestamp of 14:16 UTC, correlating with the patch window.
2. SSH'd into runner VMs and checked gitlab-runner service:
   systemctl status gitlab-runner — service was running but logs showed TLS    connection timeout to gitlab.internal.company.com.
3. Tested connectivity: curl -v https://gitlab.internal.company.com — connection    refused, confirming firewall block.
4. Reviewed nftables rules: nft list ruleset — confirmed the outbound ACCEPT rule    for port 443 was missing after the package update reset the ruleset.
5. Re-added the required outbound rule on all 8 runner VMs:
   nft add rule inet filter output tcp dport 443 accept.
6. Persisted the rule: nft list ruleset > /etc/nftables.conf.
7. Restarted gitlab-runner: systemctl restart gitlab-runner.
8. Confirmed runners came online in GitLab admin panel within 60 seconds.
9. Monitored pipeline queue — all 120 pending jobs began processing within 5 min.
10. Updated the Ansible firewall role to include idempotent nftables rules for     runner hosts so future OS patches cannot reset required rules.

PREVENTIVE MEASURES:
Added a post-patch Ansible job in Satellite to validate runner connectivity immediately after any OS patching task.

short_description: Red Hat Satellite 6 content sync failing — repository metadata error
description: Red Hat Satellite 6.14 server SATELLITE-PROD-01 is failing to sync content from the Red Hat CDN for RHEL 8 BaseOS and AppStream repositories. Error: 'Metadata checksum mismatch'. Monthly patching cycle starts in 3 days and content must be current.
close_notes:
ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS:
The Satellite server's /var/lib/pulp partition ran out of space (100% utilization) during a previous partial sync, leaving corrupted/incomplete metadata files in the Pulp content store. Subsequent sync attempts failed checksum validation against the CDN because the local metadata was incomplete.

RESOLUTION STEPS TAKEN:
1. Checked Satellite task details in the WebUI → Monitor → Tasks to identify    the exact failed sync tasks and error messages.
2. Confirmed disk usage: df -h /var/lib/pulp — showed 100% usage.
3. Identified old content view versions that were no longer in use:
   hammer content-view version list --organization 'Default Organization'.
4. Deleted 8 old content view versions to reclaim space:
   hammer content-view version delete --id <id> --organization 'Default Org'.
5. Ran Pulp orphan cleanup to remove unreferenced content:
   foreman-rake katello:delete_orphaned_content.
6. Reclaimed 85 GB; partition now at 52% utilization.
7. Cleared the corrupted metadata by removing the affected repository directory    under /var/lib/pulp/content/yum and re-initiating the sync.
8. Triggered a fresh sync for RHEL 8 BaseOS and AppStream repositories from the    Satellite WebUI — both completed successfully.
9. Published a new content view version and promoted it to the Production lifecycle    environment for use in the upcoming patching cycle.
10. Expanded the /var/lib/pulp LVM volume by 200 GB using an available disk and     set up a Nagios alert at 70% threshold to proactively flag space issues.

PREVENTIVE MEASURES:
Automated weekly cleanup of old content view versions via a Satellite cron task.

short_description: RHEL 9 system fails to boot after kernel update
description: Production RHEL 9 server RHEL-PROD-01 failed to boot following a scheduled kernel update from 5.14.0-362 to 5.14.0-427. The system is stuck at the GRUB menu and does not proceed to the login prompt. Server hosts critical middleware services.
close_notes:
ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS:
The system failed to boot because the new kernel 5.14.0-427 introduced a breaking change with the third-party NVIDIA driver module that was installed on the host.

RESOLUTION STEPS TAKEN:
1. Accessed the GRUB menu during startup by pressing 'e' to edit the boot entry.
2. Selected the previous known-good kernel version 5.14.0-362 from the advanced    boot options menu to restore system access.
3. Logged in successfully and confirmed all services were running on the old kernel.
4. Identified the incompatible kernel module using: lsmod | grep nvidia.
5. Removed the incompatible NVIDIA driver: dnf remove nvidia-driver.
6. Locked the kernel version temporarily: dnf versionlock add kernel-5.14.0-362.
7. Set the default GRUB boot entry to the working kernel:
   grub2-set-default 'Red Hat Enterprise Linux (5.14.0-362) 9.3'.
8. Ran grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg to regenerate the bootloader config.
9. Rebooted the server and confirmed it booted into the correct kernel.
10. Opened a change request to re-test kernel 5.14.0-427 in a staging environment     before re-applying to production.

PREVENTIVE MEASURES:
Added pre-patch validation playbook in Ansible to check for third-party kernel modules before applying kernel updates.

short_description: Terraform state file lock not releasing after failed apply in Azure
description: The Terraform state file for the Azure production environment is locked and preventing any new 'terraform plan' or 'terraform apply' operations. Error: 'Error acquiring the state lock'. A previous pipeline run failed mid-apply 6 hours ago and the lock was never released. Infrastructure changes are blocked.
close_notes:
ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS:
A Terraform apply pipeline run failed mid-execution due to an Azure API timeout while provisioning a new subnet. The Terraform process was killed abruptly by the CI runner timeout (30-minute limit), which prevented the normal state unlock procedure from running at the end of the process.

RESOLUTION STEPS TAKEN:
1. Confirmed the state lock by running: terraform plan — output showed lock ID,    lock holder info, and timestamp from 6 hours prior.
2. Noted the Lock ID from the error output (e.g., abc123-def456-...).
3. Reviewed Azure Storage Account (where remote state backend resides) to confirm    the lease status on the tfstate blob via Azure portal — showed 'Leased'.
4. Verified no active Terraform process was still running in any CI pipeline.
5. Forced-released the state lock using:
   terraform force-unlock -force <LOCK-ID>.
6. Confirmed lock was released: ran terraform plan again — no lock error.
7. Reviewed the partially applied state to identify any resources in an    inconsistent state using: terraform show.
8. Ran terraform apply -target=azurerm_subnet.prod_subnet to complete the    interrupted resource creation safely.
9. Verified full infrastructure state was consistent with the plan.
10. Updated the CI pipeline configuration to increase timeout to 60 minutes and     added a post-failure hook to run terraform force-unlock automatically on     pipeline failure with the lock ID captured from stderr.

PREVENTIVE MEASURES:
Added Sentinel policy in Terraform Cloud to alert when a state lock is held longer than 45 minutes without activity.

short_description: ArgoCD application out of sync after Kubernetes RBAC policy change
description: ArgoCD on the production Kubernetes cluster shows 15 applications in 'OutOfSync' status following a security team RBAC policy change that restricted the ArgoCD service account permissions. ArgoCD cannot reconcile application state and deployments are blocked.
close_notes:
ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS:
The security team applied a new ClusterRole policy that removed the 'create' and 'patch' verbs from the ArgoCD application controller service account for the core/v1 'secrets' resource. This was part of a least-privilege hardening initiative but was applied without consulting the ArgoCD platform team.

RESOLUTION STEPS TAKEN:
1. Reviewed ArgoCD application controller logs:
   kubectl logs -n argocd deployment/argocd-application-controller | grep 'error'.
2. Identified 'secrets is forbidden: User cannot patch resource secrets' errors    indicating RBAC permission denial.
3. Retrieved the current ArgoCD ClusterRole:
   kubectl get clusterrole argocd-application-controller -o yaml.
4. Compared against the required permissions from the official ArgoCD RBAC docs.
5. Coordinated with the security team to review the specific permissions required    and agreed on the minimum needed set.
6. Patched the ClusterRole to restore 'create' and 'patch' on 'secrets' with a    resource name restriction limiting scope to ArgoCD-managed namespaces only.
7. Applied the updated RBAC manifest:
   kubectl apply -f argocd-clusterrole-updated.yaml.
8. Triggered a manual refresh on all 15 OutOfSync applications from the ArgoCD UI.
9. Confirmed all applications transitioned to 'Synced' and 'Healthy' status.
10. Documented the ArgoCD required RBAC permissions in the internal security     baseline document and added ArgoCD as a stakeholder in future RBAC change     review meetings.

PREVENTIVE MEASURES:
Added ArgoCD RBAC validation as a step in the Kubernetes security hardening change approval workflow.

short_description: Jenkins pipeline failing with 'No space left on device' error
description: Jenkins master node JENKINS-MASTER-01 is reporting 'No space left on device' errors across all running pipelines. All builds are failing at the checkout stage. Development teams cannot merge or deploy code.
close_notes:
ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS:
The Jenkins workspace directory /var/lib/jenkins/workspace consumed all available disk space (500 GB partition) due to accumulated build artifacts and workspace directories from abandoned pipeline runs over the past 90 days.

RESOLUTION STEPS TAKEN:
1. Confirmed disk usage via: df -h and du -sh /var/lib/jenkins/* to identify the    largest directories consuming space.
2. Identified 230 GB of stale workspace directories from deleted or renamed jobs.
3. Stopped Jenkins service gracefully: systemctl stop jenkins.
4. Manually deleted stale workspace directories:
   rm -rf /var/lib/jenkins/workspace/<stale-job-names>.
5. Cleared the Jenkins build history archive older than 30 days from the UI.
6. Removed old .hpi and .jpi plugin backup files from /var/lib/jenkins/plugins.
7. Freed 280 GB of disk space; partition now at 44% utilization.
8. Restarted Jenkins: systemctl start jenkins and verified all pipelines resumed.
9. Configured Workspace Cleanup Plugin on all existing jobs to wipe workspace    before each build via job DSL update.
10. Set up a cron job to run a disk-space alert script when usage exceeds 75%:
    0 6 * * * /usr/local/bin/disk_alert.sh >> /var/log/disk_alert.log 2>&1.

PREVENTIVE MEASURES:
Implemented Jenkins Configuration as Code (JCasC) policy to enforce workspace cleanup and artifact retention for a maximum of 10 builds.

short_description: OpenShift pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff after namespace resource quota exceeded
description: Multiple pods in the 'payments-prod' namespace on OCP-CLUSTER-PROD are stuck in CrashLoopBackOff state. The payments processing service is unavailable, impacting all customer transactions. Alert triggered at 02:14 UTC.
close_notes:
ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS:
The namespace resource quota for CPU requests was set to 8 cores. A recent Helm chart update accidentally doubled the CPU requests per pod replica from 500m to 1000m, causing new pods to fail scheduling and existing pods to be evicted.

RESOLUTION STEPS TAKEN:
1. Identified failing pods: oc get pods -n payments-prod | grep CrashLoopBackOff.
2. Described the resource quota: oc describe resourcequota -n payments-prod to    confirm CPU limits were breached.
3. Reviewed recent Helm deployment history:
   helm history payments-service -n payments-prod.
4. Rolled back the Helm release to the previous revision:
   helm rollback payments-service 14 -n payments-prod.
5. Confirmed pods transitioned from CrashLoopBackOff to Running state within 3    minutes of rollback.
6. Verified all payment transactions resumed by checking application logs and    monitoring dashboards in Grafana.
7. Temporarily increased namespace CPU quota to 16 cores as a buffer:
   oc patch resourcequota compute-resources -n payments-prod    --patch '{"spec":{"hard":{"requests.cpu":"16"}}}'.
8. Fixed the Helm chart values file to restore CPU requests to 500m per pod.
9. Deployed the corrected chart through the proper CI pipeline with peer review.
10. Restored namespace quota back to 8 cores after confirming stable operation     for 2 hours post-deployment.

PREVENTIVE MEASURES:
Added a pre-deployment OPA/Gatekeeper policy to reject Helm values where CPU requests per pod exceed a defined threshold.

short_description: Podman containers on RHEL 8 unable to pull images from private registry
description: Developers on RHEL 8 workstations and dev servers report that 'podman pull' commands fail with 'x509: certificate signed by unknown authority' when pulling from the internal container registry registry.internal.company.com:5000.
close_notes:
ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS:
The internal container registry SSL certificate was renewed 3 days ago. The new certificate was signed by an updated internal CA that had not been distributed to RHEL 8 hosts. Podman uses the system certificate trust store, which did not contain the new CA certificate.

RESOLUTION STEPS TAKEN:
1. Reproduced the error: podman pull registry.internal.company.com:5000/myimage:latest
   Output: Error: x509: certificate signed by unknown authority.
2. Retrieved the new internal CA certificate from the PKI team:
   company-internal-ca-v2.crt.
3. Copied the CA cert to the system trust anchor directory on affected hosts:
   cp company-internal-ca-v2.crt /etc/pki/ca-trust/source/anchors/.
4. Updated the system-wide CA trust store:
   update-ca-trust extract.
5. Verified the cert was added: trust list | grep 'Company Internal CA'.
6. Re-tested: podman pull registry.internal.company.com:5000/myimage:latest —    pull succeeded.
7. Rolled out the fix to all 35 affected RHEL 8 hosts using an Ansible ad-hoc    playbook targeting the 'rhel8-dev' inventory group.
8. Confirmed resolution across all hosts by running a post-task smoke test.
9. Notified all development teams that image pulls were restored.
10. Raised a change request to include new CA distribution in the standard     certificate renewal runbook and Satellite content view.

PREVENTIVE MEASURES:
Integrated CA certificate deployment into the PKI renewal pipeline via Ansible Tower so hosts are updated automatically on next renewal.

short_description: Ansible Tower job template failing with SSH authentication error on RHEL hosts
description: Ansible Tower job templates targeting RHEL 8 hosts are failing with 'Permission denied (publickey)' SSH errors since 08:00 UTC today. Automated patching and configuration management workflows are blocked for 47 servers.
close_notes:
ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS:
The SSH private key stored in the Ansible Tower credential object expired and was rotated by the Secrets Management team (CyberArk) as part of a scheduled 90-day key rotation policy. The Tower credential was not updated with the new key.

RESOLUTION STEPS TAKEN:
1. Reviewed Tower job output logs to confirm the exact error:    'Permission denied (publickey,gssapi-keyex,gssapi-with-mic)'.
2. Tested manual SSH from Tower host using the stored key:
   ssh -i /tmp/test_key ansible-svc@rhel-host-01 — confirmed key rejection.
3. Contacted the Secrets Management team to retrieve the newly rotated SSH private    key for the 'ansible-svc' service account from CyberArk vault.
4. Logged into Ansible Tower UI → Credentials → 'RHEL-Prod-SSH-Cred'.
5. Updated the SSH Private Key field with the new key provided by Secrets team.
6. Saved the credential and re-launched a test job template against one host.
7. Confirmed successful SSH authentication and task execution on the test host.
8. Re-launched all failed job templates; all 47 hosts completed successfully.
9. Documented the key rotation schedule in the runbook and set a calendar reminder    for 85 days hence to proactively update the Tower credential before expiry.
10. Raised a request to integrate Ansible Tower with CyberArk API for automated     credential sync on rotation events to prevent recurrence.

PREVENTIVE MEASURES:
CyberArk-to-Tower dynamic credential sync integration scheduled for Q3.

short_description: Prometheus alertmanager not sending alerts — SMTP relay misconfiguration
description: The Prometheus Alertmanager has silently stopped delivering email and PagerDuty alerts for the past 18 hours. On-call team only discovered the issue when manually checking dashboards. Multiple critical alerts for CPU, memory, and disk thresholds were missed during this window.
close_notes:
ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS:
A network team change rotated the internal SMTP relay server from smtp-relay-01.internal to smtp-relay-02.internal without updating the Alertmanager configuration. Additionally, the PagerDuty integration key was regenerated during a PagerDuty account audit, invalidating the existing key in Alertmanager config.

RESOLUTION STEPS TAKEN:
1. Checked Alertmanager UI at http://alertmanager:9093 — showed a backlog of 47    firing alerts with 'error sending notification' status.
2. Reviewed Alertmanager logs:
   kubectl logs -n monitoring deployment/alertmanager | grep 'error'.
3. Found two distinct errors: SMTP 'connection refused' to old relay, and    PagerDuty '403 Forbidden' due to invalid integration key.
4. Retrieved the new SMTP relay hostname from the network team's change record.
5. Retrieved the new PagerDuty integration key from the PagerDuty service settings.
6. Updated the Alertmanager ConfigMap in Kubernetes:
   kubectl edit configmap alertmanager-config -n monitoring.
7. Changed smtp_smarthost to smtp-relay-02.internal:25 and updated the    pagerduty_configs service_key field.
8. Reloaded Alertmanager config without restart by sending SIGHUP:
   curl -X POST http://alertmanager:9093/-/reload.
9. Sent a test alert using amtool and confirmed email and PagerDuty delivery.
10. Reviewed all 47 missed alerts manually, triaged any that required action, and     confirmed no production incidents were missed during the outage window.

PREVENTIVE MEASURES:
Added a synthetic alerting canary — a always-firing 'watchdog' alert that pages on-call if no heartbeat alert is received within 5 minutes, ensuring alerting pipeline health is continuously validated.
